text: SFL-corporasjon har en impressiv trakkrekord i maritimindustrinet,
spørre nødvendigheden hver kvart siden det er en listning på New York Stock Exchange i 2004.
The company's fleet of more than 80 vessels is split between tankers, bulkers, container vessels,
and offshore assets. The impact of COVID-19 on global trade has created challenges and hopefully
opportunities as well, and in this update we'll discuss the outlook for SFL and its markets.
Welcome to Utbitte, the D&B podcast where we explain the financial markets and the global economy.
My name is Marius Brunhaugen from D&B Markets and today I'm joined by Axel Olesen,
the CFO of SFL Corporation. Welcome Axel, how are you?
Thanks Marius, I'm doing very well and thank you for having me as a guest on your podcast.
Great, looking forward to this update for sure. We're also joined by the D&B Markets analyst,
shipping analyst, Matt, be everything in ship shape, Matt?
Yes, certainly Marius, everything is in ship shape as you call it.
That's good, we'll do this the way that you and I will take turns asking questions to Axel today
and I'll kick off and we choose to enter this podcast by picking straight to the hornest nest,
because the global pandemic has created a very challenging situation for many shipping and offshore
markets. Dry bulk for eksempel has been below cash break even for most of 2020.
Container vessels have seen increased idling and recently falling rates,
while the outlook for offshore has certainly not gotten more promising.
So how are you working with your customers in this situation Axel?
Oh you know these are indeed unprecedented times and I'm very pleased and impressed to see
how the SFL team has addressed the situation over the last few months.
For us at SFL the safety of our seafarers on board our vessels is top priority and
to minimize any spread of the COVID-19 disease, you already in January implemented stronger measures
through any contagion among our crew members. We're hence very grateful that there has
been no contagion board or vessels to this date. Moreover, we continuously monitor the situation
to get with our counterparties and fleet managers and there's been no material business interruptions
in the first quarter and so far in the second quarter. On the revenue side all the clients are
current on their payments and no shipping counterparties have requested charter amendments
and since 90% of SFL's revenue is coming from long term charter contracts,
the company is relatively insulated from the reasonable utility in the shipping and offshore markets.
That said we will of course continue to closely monitor developments in our customers and markets
in order to be able to react quickly to any potential business disruption
and at the same time we look for new business opportunities
as we believe the reasonable utility also represents an interesting entry point in many segments.
Axel, you said no customers has asked for amendment passiviet, but it's
Seedrill has initiated discussion with its creditors and you have three drilling rigs on lease to Seedrill
and you have about 600 million associated bank debt to these three rigs.
Can you explain a bit how you see a Seedrill restructuring could affect SFL corporation?
Absolutt. As I said no shipping counterparties have requested charter amendments,
but there has been some questions relating to the rigs we have on charter to Seedrill
and up until 2017 the offshore segment also a largest segment for a long period,
but it's now down to 25% of our charter backlog.
With SFL we firmly believe that our offshore drilling exposure is manageable
as we have two good rigs working contributing strong cash growth to Seedrill.
Two of the rigs are harsh environment units working in the North Sea
with West Linus subcharter to Conoco Phillips until the end of 2028,
while the West Hercules is subcharter to Equinor until next year.
The third rig with Taurus is idle and laid up in Norway.
Seedrill is paying big reach charter hire on all these rigs
and we continue reducing the debt on the rigs as per scheduled
and given the high attention to the offshore segment currently
we would like to highlight the significant deleveraging
that has taken place the past three years
where we have reduced debt with more than 30% on these rigs
and of the overall 600 million of bank debt SFL is guaranteeing less than 50%.
As for the free cash flow contribution from the rigs after debt service
the total contribution from our rigs on charter to Seedrill is 7 cents per share
but importantly the contribution is individual per rig
and the idle rig with Taurus is only represents approximately 2 cents per quarter
which is very manageable for SFL.
So in terms of potential restructuring we believe it will be in the interest
of all stakeholders to have a financially stronger counterpart
and we are in a constructive dialogue with Seedrill to find a sustainable path
going forward.
Our trick for the rigs that are idle which includes the West Taurus.
But for now Seedrill keeps paying the higher on schedule
and at the end of the first quarter they had more than a billion dollars in cash.
And then on the dividend side you decided in the last quarter to cut the dividend
from 35 cents per share each quarter on common equity to 25 cents per share.
Can you share some can you give some background behind this decision
and is it at any point related to expectations of reduced higher for instance
on the Seedrill and West Taurus?
You know so far our business has not been materially impacted by the general market
disruption caused by the COVID-19 pandemic.
However the board believed it was prudent to review the dividend payout deal for SFL.
With a focus on maintaining a strong balance sheet and investing capacity over the next few quarters
in a setting with more expected market volatility.
And while reported income for the first quarter was negative due to some non-cash accounting
impairments on assets and swaps.
The underlying business was robust and these impairments did not have any impact
on destructible cash flow from our assets.
However if we try to break down the dividend adjustment there are a few considerations going into this.
We have already addressed Seedrill and the current contribution from the West Taurus
being approximately two cents per quarter.
And looking at the dry bulk segment where we have nine hand-sized vessels trading in the
short-term market I would say that the expectations for the dry bulk has changed.
Hence the change and so that they adjusted the projections for these vessels with
approximately two cents per quarter.
And the same goes for a two-fee the container vessels and two car carriers
trading in the short-term market with another two cents per quarter.
And finally you want to make sure that SFL is a really robust entity with ample investment capacity.
And right now I believe having capacity to invest is a real strength especially in an
environment where you can find interesting investment opportunities that are long-term
accretive to the company.
Axel, I think you have been paying dividends for 65 consecutive quarters.
Are you confident that you will be able to continue paying dividends each quarter going forward?
Yeah I know as a general policy we do not guide on the dividend as that's up to
that's determined by the board on a quarterly basis.
However if we look into some of the factors that go into the process of determining the dividend
I believe we are in a good position to continue the quarterly dividend payments to investors
also going forward.
With a long history of more than 15 years this is not the first time the company has experienced
volatile markets and we have been able to navigate these markets very well in the past due
to the way we approach risk management.
We have a conservative approach in underwriting new business
by only acquiring assets backed by long-term charter contracts.
This is reflected in the strong cash flow visibility from our fixed chart rate backlog
which currently stands at approximately 3.6 billion
which contributes approximately 90% of our revenue on a quarterly basis.
We have a strong cash balance.
We have more than 200 million of freely available cash at the end of Q1
and with a very supportive bank group with close to 30 international banks
we are able to secure attractive financing even in the volatile market.
Global interest rates have seen another push towards zero with the central banks
initiating quantitative easing efforts.
SFL common equity is yielding close to 10%.
How is lower interest rates supporting the valuation of SFL?
Although global interest rates have come down
at the same time risk premiums in high yield and entry markets have increased
and of course our stock being in that segment has come down accordingly.
And we really believe it's up to the market to decide our share price
and of course implied dividend yield.
I mean our focus is to work very hard to increase the backlog and cash flow
and I believe with the decreased cost of capital we're seeing
we are able to create what I call long-term shareholder value
by doing increase the transactions.
And in recent years we've seen a flight to quality trend among maritime lending banks
meaning companies regarded as strong credits
has really seen declining loan margins.
For SFL this has meant that we've gotten access to more pockets of capital
at the declining cost of capital.
As a recent example in the middle of the COVID-19 pandemic in April
we raised 175 million of debt for container vessels
at an all-time low interest margin, low 1.9% including liber for five years.
So very attractive terms.
And touching a bit more on the valuation side
you announced an ATM program on 14th of May
a program that allows you to raise up to 100 million in equity.
If you look at our latest update on SFL corp
we estimated the sum of the park valuation of 8.4 dollars per share
and if you take the yesterday closing price
you can say that common equity is now trading at the price to some of the park of 1.25.
It's probably hard to guide but would you consider tapping the equity market at these levels
or do you use this as a tool when it's even more accretive to the existing shareholders?
You know, I mean if you look at our cash balance at the end of the first quarter
that was more than 200 million.
So as I say I mean the ATM program is mainly a tool in the financial toolbox
and the timing of the filing of the ATM program was really a housekeeping item
in connection with the renewal of the shelf registration.
That said you know issuing equity may be appropriate from time to time
from a risk management point of view
but our focus is also always to maintain a capital structure
that we are seeing creates the most value for equity and bondholders.
As Mats alluded to, do you see any interesting growth opportunities
coming out of the pandemic?
Yeah you know that the increased risk premium seen in financial markets
have definitely been reflected in asset markets where we operate.
I mean the recent VLCC transactions we did with the language group
is a good example of what can be done in today's environment.
We are very comfortable with the entry value of 65 million for a new VLCC
and with a non-call tree structure
our equity part is more or less fully paid back during the initial three years
period of that charter.
So for us I mean we continue to be on the outlook for kind of the bread and butter
shipping transactions in line in dry walk and thank you segments
but we also keep an eye open on interesting opportunities
in other related maritime sectors.
We're not saying that we'll reinvent the SFL deal
but we think it's worthwhile to take a look at some of the asset classes
that previously attracted too cheap capital
versus felt that value in the past.
All right, thank you so much Axel for giving us this update.
It will be interesting to follow you going forward for sure.
Thank you also to you Mats for joining us and finally thanks to everyone for listening.
Innollet skal helle ikke anses for å være investeringsrådgivning
tilpasset den enkelte kundesbehovet.
Information som gis i podcasten er kun menet som generell spareværledning
og er ikke ment å utgjøre juridisk, finansiell, kommerkjell,
skattemessig eller regnskapsmessig rådgivning.
Innollet sendingen er ikke gjennomgått eller godkjent av
DMB Markets avdeling for analyset.
Alle uttaleser reflektere DMBs vurderinger på tidspunktet for den
aktuelle sendingen og kan i etterkant endres utnærmere beskjed.
DMB er ikke ansvarlig for hvordan informasjonen i podcasten benyttes eller tolkes.
Informasjonen som henviser til historisk avkassning i finansielle
instrumenter, en finansiell indeks eller prognoser,
gir ingen garanti for fremtidig avkassning.
DMB gjør oppmerksom på at den hver investering i et finansiellst
instrument er forbundet med risiko for økonomisk kap.
Finansielle instrumenter kan både øke og minnske valg,
slik at avkassning kan bli negativ som følge av kursvalg.
DMB tar innted ansvar for direkte eller indirekte tap
og kostnader som måtte oppstå ved bruk av den informasjonen som gis i podcasten.
